summary: Simon Späti examines why mid-to-large enterprises struggle to adopt the Modern Data Stack (MDS), despite its open-source advantages. He traces the typical enterprise journey from custom scripts and legacy systems through the technology adoption lifecycle, noting that MDS tools are still in their 'early market' phase. The post contrasts US startup culture's rapid adoption with European enterprises' focus on cost, privacy, and regulatory compliance. While acknowledging the complexity and integration challenges of modular MDS tools, Späti argues that open-source solutions offer enterprises significant opportunities in flexibility, legal compliance, and competitive advantage—provided they have the technical capacity to manage them.
key_points: Enterprise data environments are inherently complex due to multiple teams, legacy systems, technical debt from departed employees, and the need to integrate ERP, CRM, and various departmental tools
The Modern Data Stack is still in its 'early market' phase—most tools are brand new and haven't crossed the chasm to mainstream enterprise adoption
European enterprises differ significantly from US startups in their adoption patterns due to stricter privacy regulations (GDPR), cost optimization focus, and historical vendor lock-in experiences
Open-source MDS provides a game-changing opportunity for smaller companies to outperform larger enterprises with data-driven strategies and modern technology

The tradeoff between modularity and complexity is real: integrating good tools doesn't guarantee a good stack, and enterprises must honestly assess whether they have capacity to own and manage the stack themselves
money_quotes: My colleagues and I have essentially re-implemented the MDS repeatedly at each company in one way or another.

Today you get the full data stack for free, open-sourced on GitHub. It's a game-changer and is one reason that small companies can outperform large enterprises regularly with strong data-driven strategies.
Working with data within a big organization is complicated and unpredictable. On the surface, it might not look that challenging.
Integrating good tools doesn't mean you'll get a good stack and expected results. It's still a complex thing.
Large enterprises typically ignore the trends until they are unavoidable.
Your final choices have long-term consequences, as you lock the company into that proprietary tool.
Ask yourself: 'Do we have the capacity and knowledge to own and manage the data stack ourselves?'
key_insight: The Modern Data Stack offers enterprises a path to escape vendor lock-in and gain competitive advantage, but adoption requires honest assessment of organizational capacity—integrating good tools doesn't automatically produce a good stack.
